title: Tropical psi Classes and Tropicalizations of psi Classes
speaker: Renzo Cavalieri
abstract: The general rule for the interactions between tropical geometry and moduli spaces of course is the following: everything you may wish is going to work like a charm in genus zero, and break down horribly in higher genus. This is the case for the tautological intersection theory of psi classes, a class of fundamental objects in the geometry of moduli spaces of curves: the generating function of their intersection numbers has made waves, pun intended, when it was noticed that it is a tau function for the KdV hierarchy. Back to tropical geometry: in genus zero tropical psi classes have been first defined by Mikhalkin in the early 2000's, then through the work of Kerber-Markwig and Katz it was shown that intersection numbers of tropical psi classes agree with their algebraic counterparts. In work with A.Gross and H.Markwig (2021), we were able to make sense of tropical psi classes in higher genus, by making the tropical moduli space of curves into a stack for families of tropical curves with an affine structure. This is a combinatorial theory that recovers the algebraic intersection numbers, but can also produce results that do not have a counter part in algebraic geometry. To this end, in recent work with A.Gross we answer the question of when we can show that tropical psi classes are tropicalizations. In order to even make sense of the statement, we had to introduce a notion of tropicalization for families of curves based on the Picard theory of the base.
tags: Workshop on Combinatorics of Enumerative Geometry

proof_stages.label: Algebraic moduli of curves and psi classes
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up \(\overline M_{g,n}\) as a stratified/toroidal moduli space with gluing and forgetful morphisms, then defines algebraic psi classes as first Chern classes of cotangent, equivalently conormal, line bundles along marked sections.

proof_stages.label: Tropical curves and tropical moduli spaces
proof_stages.summary: Tropical curves are introduced as metric graphs with vertex genera, and \(M^{\mathrm{trop}}_{g,n}\) is described as a generalized cone complex corresponding to the boundary-complex combinatorics of \(overline M_{g,n}\).

proof_stages.label: Genus-zero benchmark and higher-genus gap
proof_stages.summary: The speaker recalls that in genus zero \(\overline M_{0,n}\) is a tropical compactification whose toric comparison explains tropical intersection theory, while in higher genus analogous statements are much more recent and incomplete.

proof_stages.label: Combinatorial tropical psi classes via tropical families
proof_stages.summary: Prior work defines families of tropical curves with affine structures, builds tropical line bundles and operational tropical psi classes from conormal data of sections, and leaves open whether these classes are genuine tropicalizations of algebraic psi classes.

proof_stages.label: Affine tropicalization of toroidal schemes
proof_stages.summary: The recent work associates to a toroidal scheme an extended cone complex equipped with an affine-function sheaf modeled on principal boundary divisors, with functoriality, log-modification invariance, and compatibility with cross ratios.

proof_stages.label: Tropicalization criterion for line bundles
proof_stages.summary: A line bundle tropicalizes to a tropical line bundle, i.e. an affine-function torsor, precisely when it is a boundary line bundle and is combinatorially principal near the relevant cone or stratum at infinity.

proof_stages.label: Tropicalized curve families and conormal torsors near sections
proof_stages.summary: A family of algebraic curves gives a map of tropical spaces which need only satisfy the tropical-family axioms near the marked section, where a slope-one affine torsor encodes the tropicalization of the conormal bundle under the same combinatorial-principality condition.

proof_stages.label: Psi-class tropicalization theorem and consequences
proof_stages.summary: When the slope-one piecewise-linear function near the section is combinatorially principal, the previously defined tropical psi class equals the tropicalization of the algebraic psi class, clarifying both the correspondence theorem and the limits of what tropical geometry sees without extra input.

examples.name: Running example: \(\overline M_{1,2}\) and \(M^{\mathrm{trop}}_{1,2}\)
examples.note: The genus-one, two-marked moduli space is used to make the algebraic strata, gluing and forgetful maps, the tropical cone complex with a folded cone, and the slope detected by a tropical psi class concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses Mbar_{1,2}/M^{trop}_{1,2} as a running case, explicitly analyzing its strata, gluing and forgetful maps, enumerating tropical cones, and explaining the folded cone via edge-length symmetry.

examples.name: Genus-zero moduli as a tropical compactification
examples.note: The case \(\overline M_{0,n}\) illustrates the familiar setting where a torus embedding, fan, and toric compactification supply global linear functions and a successful tropical intersection comparison.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker traces the genus-zero construction: embed M_{0,n} in a torus, tropicalize to a fan, build the toric variety, close up to get Mbar_{0,n}, and explains resulting intersection-theoretic consequences.

passes.reason: Genus zero is the minimal/tree-like case of the curve-moduli story, used as the well-behaved model rather than to refute a claim.
genus zero is the minimal tree-like genus case, used as the positive case where tropical compactification works
genus-zero moduli are the minimal-genus/base-case end of the curve-moduli hierarchy and are used as a positive tropical-compactification case
Genus-zero moduli is the minimal-genus/base case of the curve-moduli tower, used as a positive tropical-compactification model.
genus zero is the minimal cycle-free end of the stable-curve hierarchy and is cited as the positive tropical-compactification case

Genus zero is the minimal, tree-like/no-cycle end of the moduli-of-curves hierarchy and is used as the positive case where tropical compactification works.
genus-zero moduli form the minimal-genus, tree-like base case where the tropical compactification picture works.
used as the positive genus-zero model where tropical compactification works, with genus zero the minimal rational base case

examples.name: Pencil of plane cubics through eight points
examples.note: This previous computation tropicalizes a pencil of cubics to a cover of \(M^{\mathrm{trop}}_{1,1}\) and checks that the tropical psi class gives the classical value \(1/24\).
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker says one tropicalizes a pencil of cubics through eight points to obtain a cover of M^{trop}_{1,1} and computes the psi class, giving the matching 1/24-type answer.

examples.name: Genus-one correction by feeding in \(\psi_1\) on \(\overline M_{1,1}\)
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker sketches genus one as a plausible test case for augmenting tropical intersection theory with the minimal non-boundary algebraic information needed to reconstruct psi classes.
examples.engagement_reason: The genus-one idea of feeding in psi_1 on Mbar_{1,1} is discussed speculatively as needed extra information, not actually constructed or computed in the talk.

passes.reason: The class on the minimal genus-one pointed moduli space is used as base correction data, not as a counterexample.
